speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three with survivor David Klebanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the group of people now.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right the-- what? You're going to tell me about the group of people. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Yeah, I wanted to finish, nevertheless. I came, and I wanted to join, but I was told, no, the number is already crossed, no more accepted. These 300 people were brought to a place outside of the city and killed. It was only protected for this. That was not only in Kaunas. So it was in Riga. So it was in VIlna So it was in all cities, to destroy the intelligent part, [INAUDIBLE] part of the population. And then there were doctors, teachers, engineers, et cetera, you know, to work in all this. 300 people in Kaunas, which for a Jewish population is quite a lot, the same day were brought and killed-- [? liquidated. ?]

words.text: me about the group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, I was-- I cannot work in medical. Too many doctor are still in ghetto. In [NON-ENGLISH], I'm not-- not [NON-ENGLISH] Kaunas. I'm going to work every day to the airport.

speaker: interviewer
text: A doctor went to work in the airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that is right, with a spade, too. We were working to enlarge the airport in-- in Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, for a while, I was a simple worker. But then, you know, they realized that they need somebody to take care if something happens. So finally, I became a doctor who-- not to work with the spade, you know-- and-- and just if somebody got wounded, you know, and needed, you know, medical help. Plus, it is-- it was in such a shift. Shift is correct, [? in English? ?] There were around 1,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of the people in Kaunas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in that city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, three-- three, it was. The Jewish-- the Jews were speaking Jewish, were speaking--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. They were speaking Russian. And they were speaking Lithuanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were a very intelligent, educated person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whom? Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You.

speaker: interviewee
text: I consider myself as very well-educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that period of time, did you ever think or imagine what Hitler would-- would end up doing? You must've been among intelligent Jews, as well. Did you discuss it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, you know-- we were-- after this-- this-- this intelligence action-- action, for a while, it was quiet. We were going to work. We had some rations-- not enough, always hungry. In October-- I will not say exactly on the 13th of October or the 14th of October-- we were ordered to come to a big, empty place in this where we lived-- big empty place.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] This was 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941, October 14-- October 14th or 15th. And then just a few Germans were there, you know, and ask us to parade, by families, one family, two families to parade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this? Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am with my wife. And there was also her aunt. And we were three persons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many other people were you? How many other people were asked to participate?

speaker: interviewee
text: 33,000 people. And right away, we're divided with the whole family with kids to the left, without kids, depends. If it is old people, to the left. Younger people to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it anyone in particular? Nothing with the higher-- higher-- higher degrees. Sergeant, I don't recall. Probably a sergeant, I don't know-- but nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so 15,000 people were divided right away into one group. 18,000 into other group. And we, the 15,000, were asked to go home. The 18 were taken. They stayed in one place. And the next day, gathered and brought to a Tenth Fort. I told you about Seventh Fort. That is Tenth Fort, approximately two, three miles from our place. And from the moment we arrived, we started to hear [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shots, screaming, terrible screaming, which is still in my ears, of this 18,000 people as they realized they are being brought here to be killed. And [? always, ?] it lasted almost two days to kill these 18 people of Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: 18,000 People?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18,000 People. It was a same thing done in Vilna This was the same thing done in-- in-- and on different days-- in-- in-- in Riga. And in Riga, it was done on a Sunday. It was done on a Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you tell me about what you saw. You were there in Kaunas. I'm telling you [INAUDIBLE] the connection with me. I [? see-- ?] the killing was done on a Sunday-- first Sunday in December. Terrible-- I was told it was very cold. In Riga, 40,000 Jews was on this day killed. Only 3,000 was left-- 3,000 men and 300 women. How did you feel to sit together with your wife in Kaunas and hear this screaming?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. [? We didn't ?] [? want ?] it-- terrible. Terrible-- we knew what is going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think it could happen to you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, I started to-- I started to realize first. And second, I started [? to think ?] it is only for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any chance that you could escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, you know, if we-- we were stealing the German papers, you know somehow from the-- from the-- working in the city here and there. They will through out the newspapers. And here, we are reading about the [GERMAN]. It is a suburb of Berlin, There was a conference without Hitler, but the top SS men. And the Final Solution of the Jewish problem was decided.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you read what?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what was is Final Solution? All will [INAUDIBLE] be killed. How I feel felt-- so I knew-- knew that I will not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: She felt also she will not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any people in Kaunas commit suicide that you were aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not too many, but people commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a doctor, did you assisted in any of those suicides?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall doctors committing suicide. But I know people committed suicide. They knew that-- everybody realized after [GERMAN] became in the papers, that there is Final Solution of the-- of the-- the only hope was that maybe the Germans will lose the war. But it was not a hope for this. It is 1941. They are proceeding. They already took millions of Russians prisoners of war. And the-- and the-- the Russians didn't-- didn't fight them. Really, we didn't fight them. After I was liberated, a colonel, a Russian colonel-- we became very friendly-- told me, absolutely, the Russians wanted to get rid of the communist regime, but the Germans didn't take any-- any difference. They liquidated. They took around four million Russian prisoners of war. Of this half a million left. Three and a half died in-- under the worst conditions in hunger-- in hunger and cold and lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened in Kaunas after that murder, after the mass murder. How long did you remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained there till September '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December of '41, when I was at the airport, Germans came, caught just simply 300 Jewish women, and took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your wife one of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife was one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, I was informed-- the administration of the ghetto wanted to help me very much-- that they were taken to Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was there in Riga to take them to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear, we don't have any communication. We cannot write them letters. We cannot talk. We cannot-- no telephone. We are isolated-- completely isolated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any children at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I say, one day-- one day after our marriage, the Germans came. I can say no. There is a reason for divorce. Divorce, you know, in the-- in the Catholic-- [INAUDIBLE] Catholic religion, when matrimonium doctorum said non consummatum, when the marriage was given, but not consumed, that is a reason for a divorce. You understand what is consumed?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That you can say the same thing about me. I will not say-- [INAUDIBLE] almost. She is there. And we didn't know. It took a couple weeks until I was informed that she's in Riga. And then I asked the-- the people from the administration, please, if another transport goes to Riga, I want to go with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: To find her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- conditions after this were relatively quiet-- no problems, no big problems. Sometimes they will kill a Jew who smuggled too much wood to the ghetto. It was single-- single, you know, deaths, but not mass-- mass execution. And so you know, I didn't want-- I didn't want to stay in ghetto. I offered me to go as a doctor with the commander in fall, but it was July, August, to collect the-- from the fields potatoes and other vegetables. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I would [INAUDIBLE] at the head of this group. And as a doctor of this group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a few weeks later, comes one of the head from the ghetto, and say Dr. Klebanow, we have come. We have come to take you to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Kaunas ghetto. I say, if you came, probably there is going again a transport to Riga. He said, you are absolutely right. I came back. I became the head of this group, 300 mostly compelled, some who had relatives with women-- men who lost their wives, like me-- came voluntarily. And so 300 Jews, they put us on the train. And it took two or three days. We arrived at

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Riga, I found-- you know, we were brought first to ghetto. We stayed there for one day only. And then we were transported. I met my wife. I found her. And then we were transported to a old brewery, which is not far from the airport, because the people who have to work on the airport of-- of-- of the-- Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was called Spilwe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spilwe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spilwe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in that place? Was that-- that was still in the ghetto in Riga, or was that outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was outside, yes. Food would we get from ghetto to Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that from Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Riga, it was-- to go to the ghetto it was approximately three or four miles-- three or four kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in Spilwe?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Spilwe, at the beginning, 300.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soon they started to dissolve the ghetto of Riga. And they sent to us 800 people from Riga to our camp. We had-- it is an old brewery. We had plenty of space to enlarge. And 800 people came to our ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of your ghetto in Spilwe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- now, it was a big one thing, you know? Shortly after-- after I-- we are in Spilwe, people are coming to me. I see they all have temperature. For me, I suspected that it must be either typhoid fever or paratyphoid, but of this type, it is diarrhea. And I brought first one or two to the ghetto. It was ghetto still at that time. They diagnosed bacteriological that it is paratyphus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they diagnosed it, where would a person diagnose such a thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In ghetto, they have a small hospital. In ghetto they have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was it run by?

speaker: interviewee
text: By--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews. And they told me it is paratyphoid. And please, don't dare to bring more people to us. I understand that if you bring, and if the Germans know, the Germans will have one only solution. They will liquidate right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- all together, I had to know if-- right in a short time I had 20 with this-- with this sickness. I isolated. I-- I said it is a-- they have diarrheas. The Germans, we had not under-- who are under control of the Luftwaffe, of the-- of the people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell her to be quiet. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --people from-- from the Air Force. And they were very nice. Shortly after we came, came a major. And he-- he called me in. And he introduced, I am [GERMAN]. I am the [GERMAN] commandant-- commandant. And I hope that you are satisfied with the quarters what you have. I would like to give you more food, but I get rationed that food what-- what I-- I-- I get. I cannot get more. But you have warm-- warm. I supply you with-- with-- with-- with wood, no. And I realize it is a man who is a friendly man. He would--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why do you think he was prepared to do that in the middle of a war against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear, you know, why-- why-- the same thing happened with this-- with this thing, you know, what-- what-- became so famous?

speaker: interviewer
text: Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler. The same analog. Two years-- although, later we were managed by the SS. But the-- the people worked at the airport-- airport, and a still strong connection, you know, with our-- our camp. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose of your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work at the airport. Please, for them, the airports were not big enough. The-- right away-- [INAUDIBLE] [? Riga ?] was the big offensive against Leningrad. So they moved soldiers by air. And it was rather small, so they had to enlarge.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had to have enough people. The Latvians consider themselves as-- as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] friends. They didn't want to go to work, you know? So Jews worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, it is over 1,000 people, who were Jews, is working at the airport to enlarge the airport. Everything going to-- to enlarge the airport. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] With this, [NON-ENGLISH] with this, he [INAUDIBLE] who sent, you know, to manage us inside was [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he? A major?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was sergeant--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: Sergeant-- very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: But German as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the [INAUDIBLE] was managed by Obergefreiter Schumacher-- face of a murderer, but who revealed to be a very fine man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also German?

speaker: interviewee
text: All major sent us sent these men, who he selected under condition that you will be good to the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The doctor from-- [INAUDIBLE] medication, I had problem with medications. Sergeant, gets up and says, let's go to the-- to the-- [GERMAN], means small hospital, the German, at the airport. And I went there. I got acquainted with the-- the doctor. And the doctor even asked me-- invite-- invited me to a breakfast. For me, it was unusual. I was-- They addressed us not-- not the, not you-- you. They addressed us like animals. And here I see a completely different attitude.

speaker: interviewer
text: The [? air force ?] was very much still Nazi, although I ate breakfast with him-- very fine, very fine. He gave me medication, what I needed, you know? You know-- aspirins, and for diarrheas, you know the simple things, what-- what he has-- had. How many patients were you're taking care of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear, every day would come approximately each morning, among the 300 in the beginning, around 15, 20, will come for these-- these complaints. I-- I had always, you know, around 10 not working-- 10, 20 not working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these only men?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we came, men and women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the women pregnant that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very many. Not many-- few were pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- right away, I was told that if a woman-- at the beginning, it was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean? What was no problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she's pregnant. But soon, you know, the SS took over [? also. ?] And if, you know, SS gets informed that a woman is pregnant and gives birth to a child, she will be killed, and the child will be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I order, told, you know, please, whoever is pregnant, come. I will terminate the pregnancy. I'm an obstetrician gynecologist. It's my field.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were doing abortions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing abortions. I'm doing abortion not now for my camp. Not far away, was so-called [NON-ENGLISH] camp. And they were also men and women. You know, I don't recall how many-- 500, also pregnancies-- everybody, Klebanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You became the doctor responsible for the abortions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was in this respect-- it was all the [? beginning ?] army. So it was very fine attitude. But I had to do-- to do. And I will tell you, the greatest amount, it was in '44, in July. We got 2,000 Hungarian women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into mine-- Spilwe.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Spilwe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spilwe is a tremendous brewery. You have places to bring them, to give them a place where to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have barracks for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the barracks. They have straw-- straw sacks-- straw sacks. And everybody had a [INAUDIBLE]. And among this, I had around-- I announced right away, who is pregnant to come. I had 20 some pregnancies. And I recall, I worked with my wife [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing abortions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Doing abortions. Saving their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no way to hide with such a compassionate bunch of German officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: But one--

speaker: interviewer
text: One second, one second. You had these German officers working with you, supervising you, who were actually-- it seems they were quite compassionate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wouldn't they have tolerated babies? No. It is that they-- first, first, already, these political supervisions, whatever it is, it is already SS. We have the Hauptscharfuhrer, with the nickname 'the Iron Gustav.' You know when somebody comes-- comes a sergeant general, you know, it was the greatest murderer among the Nazis. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gustav Sorge was his name. Nazi nickname-- nickname, 'the Iron Gustav.' The other--

speaker: interviewer
text: He came to your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: He became commandant of our camp. Now, when I came [INAUDIBLE] first time with a pregnancy, and I came to him and I say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hauptscharfuhrer, I examined the one, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she's pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: In my camp, a pregnancy? You know, he jumped up. And he called, you know, right away, [GERMAN]. This is from the army. Come here. We have [GERMAN.]. We have-- more people are coming, you know, jokingly. But nevertheless, he says, so what do you want to do? I say, I want to terminate. Fine, I want to be present in this. All right. I cannot say no.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I-- it came, you know-- came, you know, to termination of this pregnancy, I called him and he said, I cannot come. I understand he was a womanizer, tremendous womanizer with the Latvian-- Latvian women. He was very happy that he has now a gynecologist. If something happens to these women, he will have a man who will terminate the pregnancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you two questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the fetuses that were aborted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not [? fetal-- ?] if you have an abortion, it is usually in the first three months. But for example, I had-- let me-- I will give you an example, a classical example. A classic example-- a German Jewish woman from my camp, from our camp, comes to me and she says, I'm pregnant. I examine her. She's six months pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, tell me where, why did you work? I knew already that she had some relations with the German who managed this group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a Jew. [? Floor ?] [? arbiter, ?] you know, it is-- he didn't work. He only supervised-- supervisor. And probably with him, she became pregnant. She somehow-- I don't know why-- she was hiding. But six months, she came to me. I say, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all right, we will have to terminate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was not-- not an abortion. That I had to induce labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that is-- I dilated the cervix. I ruptured the membranes. And I had between-- [INAUDIBLE] to stimulate the uterus. And I brought to-- she had contractions. She had labor. And she expelled the fetus.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fetus had died.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fetus was-- I don't-- you don't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six months-- it was too small.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't care what it is. No, it is--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean you don't care?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it weighs 600 grams, you know, what do I care? I am in a concentration camp. I know the results. I know the results that she-- if the baby is alive, and she's alive, they both will be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, know that it is-- we are in-- [INAUDIBLE] thinking completely differently. For me, I have to get rid of it. I said to put aside. What did you do with all fetal tissues?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what my sanitator, you know, did, you know? Probably went somewhere and buried-- buried them in the premises of this-- of this-- compound.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took care of the women after the abortions, as far as bleeding or hemorrhaging?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold on, I didn't finish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was starting to get a little temperature. And I decided to keep here for one, two days. I didn't know what is the reason for the temperature. Maybe she has some TB in the lungs, you know, and a temperature. But on the second day, or on the third day, I-- someone from the camp comes running to me, Visner is here. Visner is-- we are now a part of a concentration camp, Riga [NON-ENGLISH] Kaiserwald. We are a part.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when the SS people came. That is everything part of the concentration. But we were Arbeitslager. We were working, working, working for their very important part. They don't have any other workers but Jews to work on the airport to enlarge and maintain.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Visner was the-- there was-- the doctor of this Kaiserwald was a man with the name Krepsbach. He was in the rank of major in SS, Sturmbannfuhrer-- Sturmbannfuhrer Krepsbach, nickname Dr. Spritzbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spritz, you know, what means-- injection. He was known-- he was before this, he was in Mauthausen-- Camp Mauthausen. And he was with terrible reputation came. He was injecting phenol intravenously, and the terrible pains, which in ten minutes, they were dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to finish tape three. We're going to continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to continue.
